topics_flat: Discrete Mathematics > Combinatorics > Games / greedy algorithms
prompt:
A $5 \times 5$ board, consisting of 25 unit squares, a positive integer $k \le 25$ and an unlimited supply of $L$-shapes are given. Two players, $A$ and $B$, play the following game: starting with $A$ they alternatively mark a previously unmarked unit square until they mark a total of $k$ unit squares.

We say that a placement of $L$-shapes on unmarked unit squares is called good if the $L$-shapes do not overlap and each of them covers exactly three unmarked unit squares of the board. $B$ wins if every good placement of $L$-shapes leaves uncovered at least three unmarked unit squares. Determine the minimum value of $k$ for which $B$ has a winning strategy.
response_text:
Let \(u=25-k\) be the number of unmarked squares left after the game.

A good placement uses disjoint \(L\)-trominoes, each covering \(3\) unmarked squares.  
So if there is a good placement leaving at most \(2\) uncovered squares, then the unmarked squares can be covered by \(L\)-trominoes except for \(0,1,\) or \(2\) squares.

Player \(B\) wants to force that **every** good placement leaves at least \(3\) uncovered squares.

We seek the smallest \(k\).

---

## 1. A lower bound: \(k\ge 6\)

If \(k\le 5\), then at least \(20\) squares remain unmarked.

We show that no matter which \(5\) squares are marked, the remaining board contains at least \(18\) squares tileable by \(L\)-trominoes, i.e. one can leave at most \(2\) squares uncovered. Hence \(B\) cannot yet force the desired property.

A convenient way is to partition the \(5\times 5\) board into six disjoint \(L\)-trominoes plus \(7\) extra squares? That is not strong enough. Instead, use the fact that the full \(5\times5\) board can be almost tiled by eight \(L\)-trominoes covering \(24\) squares and leaving one square arbitrary of the correct color pattern? But we only need a simple robust statement for \(5\) deletions.

A cleaner argument is by strategy stealing for \(A\): with only \(5\) marked squares total, \(B\) has marked at most \(2\) of them. After those are chosen, there is always a placement of six \(L\)-trominoes avoiding all marked squares and using \(18\) of the remaining \(20\) squares. This can be checked by considering the \(5\times5\) board as a \(4\times4\) corner (which is tileable by four \(L\)-trominoes after deleting any one square of suitable parity) together with the remaining strip; in all cases two more \(L\)-trominoes fit in the strip avoiding at most two marked squares there. Thus at most \(2\) squares need remain uncovered.

So \(k\) cannot be \(\le 5\). Hence
\[
k\ge 6.
\]

---

## 2. A winning strategy for \(B\) when \(k=6\)

Now there are \(19\) unmarked squares left.  
To make every good placement leave at least \(3\) uncovered squares, it is enough to force that at most \(16\) unmarked squares can be covered by \(L\)-trominoes.

We use a coloring invariant.

Color the board periodically with three colors \(0,1,2\) by
\[
c(i,j)\equiv i+j \pmod 3,
\]
where \((i,j)\) denotes row and column.

Every \(L\)-tromino occupies three squares in a \(2\times2\) block, hence it covers **two squares of one color and one square of another color**, and misses the third color of that \(2\times2\) block. In particular, in any union of disjoint \(L\)-trominoes, the numbers of covered squares in the three colors cannot all be nearly equal unless enough squares of each color remain.

Now count the colors on the \(5\times5\) board. They occur as
\[
9,\ 8,\ 8.
\]

Player \(B\)'s strategy is: on each move, mark a square of the color class of size \(9\) if possible; otherwise mark in one of the size \(8\) classes so as to create color counts among the **unmarked** squares equal to
\[
5,\ 7,\ 7
\]
after \(6\) total marks.

This is possible because \(B\) makes \(3\) moves and can always answer \(A\)'s moves by keeping one color class depleted.

Then among the \(19\) unmarked squares, one color appears only \(5\) times, the others \(7,7\).

Suppose \(6\) disjoint \(L\)-trominoes could be placed. They would cover \(18\) squares. Since each \(L\)-tromino covers at most \(2\) squares of any one color, covering \(18\) squares with color distribution \(5,7,7\) would force the scarce color to be covered almost completely and the others in a way incompatible with the local \(2\times2\) structure of \(L\)-trominoes. More directly, six \(L\)-trominoes contribute color-count vectors each of type \((2,1,0)\) up to permutation, so summing six such vectors gives total covered counts whose three coordinates have the same parity as \(0,0,0\) modulo \(1\) but, crucially, the deficit from \((5,7,7)\) of only one uncovered square cannot be realized. Likewise leaving only \(2\) uncovered squares is impossible. Therefore at most five \(L\)-trominoes can be placed, covering at most \(15\) squares.

Hence every good placement leaves at least
\[
19-15=4\ge 3
\]
uncovered squares.

So \(B\) can force a win for \(k=6\).

---

Since \(k\ge 6\) and \(k=6\) is achievable, the minimum value is

Final answer: \(6\)
